speaker: interviewer
text: --about getting to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I finally-- in December 1946, I arrived in the United States as a-- a ward of the United States Committee for Children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was George here in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. George, by the way, wound up in Shanghai during the war. He was in the Shanghai ghetto. His story is a different story, as the-- he was interviewed by the Simon Wiesenthal Society. And it's a little different story. He did-- after the liberation, he went to Paris, where, by the way, my parents, also from Germany-- they wound up in Germany, also. And from Germany, they went to Paris. And they were joined there by my brother George and his wife. He married in Shanghai. And together they decided, after the creation of the State of Israel, to go to Israel. And they were joined by my brother Sam and [? Sheila ?] and their two children. By the way, they had one son was born in Kirghizia in Russia. And the daughter was born in the DP camp in-- in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister also? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister and her husband and child, they wound up in Paris. And they all decided together to go to Israel in 1949 after the liberation of the war, War of Liberation in Israel. And there she had another boy born in Jerusalem. And-- and-- and they all lived in Jerusalem. And my parents, again, became cafe owners in Jerusalem. And they had a nice cafe-- Cafe Sender at the [NON-ENGLISH] in Jerusalem. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were in America by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was in America by myself. And I had visited them in Israel. I joined the United States Air Force--

speaker: interviewer
text: As soon as you came?

speaker: interviewee
text: --during the Korean War.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first, I was under the auspices of the [INAUDIBLE]. I was-- they were helping me out. And then I lived with my aunt, which is my mother's sister, lived in Brooklyn. So I lived with them. And I went to high school. And I eventually went to college, Brooklyn College. And at that time, the war-- the Korean War started. And I knew that I was going to be drafted into the Army, so I decided to join the Air Force. I didn't like to be an Army-- an Army soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I joined the Air Force. And I-- I spent most of the almost four years in the Air Force in the United States. I did not wind up in Korea, thank god. And what profession did you later pick up?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- after-- after the service, I became an engineer. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What type?

speaker: interviewee
text: A mechanical design engineer. And I worked on the aerospace-- in the aerospace industry. And one of my accomplishments is that I worked on the Apollo project. And I still say that whenever I go to the Smithsonian Institute, I have my baby in the optical system that I worked on for the Apollo project. Of course, it doesn't have my name on it. But I know it-- it's my design. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you  were down in Florida working? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We worked here in-- in Long Island.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was working for a company that was involved in the Apollo project. And they sent me Boston, to Massachusetts, to work at the MIT Instrumentation Laboratory. And I worked together with the astronauts, with the big wheels. And at MIT, I was fortunate enough to work on the team that the company sent there. So we spent like three-- three years--

speaker: interviewer
text: What years?

speaker: interviewee
text: --working on-- on the--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 196-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '62-- '61 to '63--

speaker: interviewer
text: So did  you meet John Glenn--

speaker: interviewee
text: --about three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and some of the other astronauts?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, some of the other astronauts. I didn't meet John Glenn. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what's the name-- Shepard I-- I met. And as a matter of fact, it was Shepard himself that came over to my drawing desk and requested changes that we had. And, of course, we did whatever they wanted. One of the ideas that we had was like screw-held eyepieces. And he said, I'm not going to spend time unscrewing things. I want something to be able to remove quickly and easily. And we-- of course-- we designed a piece for a quick disconnect for him to be able to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was involved with the guidance systems?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the navigational system for the Apollo and the LEM, the lunar module, that landed on the moon. So I do have an autographed picture of some of the astronauts. And [INAUDIBLE]-- and they said, from the users of the equipment to the designers of the equipment. Not specifically me, because we were a whole team.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you talk about your experiences much after the war? Did people ask you in this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did. And we sort of refrained for it. It was-- for a while, it was painful to think back of-- it wasn't good times. Now, when we came back, of course, to Poland, we thought we suffered a great deal. But when we heard of the Holocaust, the [NON-ENGLISH] what happened, we sort of quieted down. We knew our suffering was not as great as the people in-- in Europe. And, of course, we found that most of our family, extended family, that remained in Warsaw was-- were perished. And they never survived. And I've since tried to reconstruct our family tree and show on the tree those who never made it and, you know, and keep a record. The kids, of course, always wanted to know what happened. And we sort of abbreviated versions for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: But now more recently, we decided to talk a little more, even amongst ourselves. My sister, my brother, and my sister-in-law would sometimes sit down. Remember this? Remember that? And I-- one does remember one thing. The other one doesn't remember. So when-- when you talk about it, it comes back. You recall certain phases of our past. And we're glad that we can put it on record, and so that the kids would remember later on. Did your experiences change the way you raised your children, do you think, or have an impact on your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we tried to raise our children in-- not Orthodox. We were all Conservative-- well, I am in the Conservative movement. I was a temple president, [NON-ENGLISH] president of my temple on Long Island, Temple Beth-El of Long Island-- of North Bellmore. And the children were involved in all the Judaic programs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are your children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My three daughters, they're all involved in  youth movement, youth group movement. My daughter, my eldest daughter spent a year at the Hebrew University in Jerusalem. My youngest one spent six months program at the Tel Aviv University. And my middle one never did get--

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my eldest daughter is Cheryl Ann. And she was born in 1955. And Janet Renee, and she was born in '58. And Penny, who is not married yet, is 1960, Penny Ellen. And my oldest daughter has two children, two boys, Michael and Daniel. And Janet has three children, Yakov, Zehava, and Eliahu. They're very-- they're strictly Orthodox. And they gave their names, only Hebrew names. They don't have any other names. And I think we tried to influence them in pursuing a-- a Jewish heritage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to Poland after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. As a program-- I was involved with the [INAUDIBLE] Jury program in Long Island. And they-- I went to Russia with my wife mainly to visit [INAUDIBLE]. And bring-- and this was in 1984-- '84 or '85-- before the-- the perestroika. And we were able to bring religious articles to them, tefillin, and tallis, and-- and books. It was pretty dangerous. But being-- first of all, being that I can speak Russian, I was able to get around a little better without going through with the group. Although, we went with the group. But I did my contacts on my own. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you go back to Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: So on the way back, we decided to stop off in Warsaw and show my wife, who happens to be an American-born, to where I used to live-- I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --just area where I used to live--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you recognize it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. The whole area where the ghetto used to be is now a big square with the monument--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --for the heroes of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where I lived is still a flat area. There's not even a building out there. And I-- I was able to point this out to my wife. We went to the Ganesha Cemetery. I was trying to find my grandparents' graves. Of course, it's impossible to find anything. It's overgrown. We spoke to the keeper there to see if there's any progress. And there's very little progress. We went to the-- to the shul where my brother, Sam, used to be the choir boy in-- in-- in the-- in that particular shul. And that's the only shul that's still standing there. There's not much of a Jewish life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's maybe a few thousand people, mostly elderly. As a matter of fact, when we went to the cemetery, the new tombstones don't even have Hebrew words on it. I mean, everything is in Polish. And I spoke to some of the elder people. They said they send children to Israel. And they are going to just live out their days over there. They didn't want to go and start a new life somewhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you have a message or any way that you feel it's affected you that you'd like to talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm a little emotional about that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Just to remember [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to remain Jewish [CRYING] and to remember the suffering, [CRYING] that we lost 6 million. [CRYING] It should not be in vain. [CRYING] Basically, that's-- that's all I have to say. OK, I'd like to introduce you-- my wife, Esther. We're married 40--

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years.

speaker: interviewee
text: 42 years. And this is my brother, Sam, who went through with us together, and his wife, Sheila. They-- they're married now 53-- [? SHEILA BORENSTEIN: ?] 53. --years. And they're-- [? SHEILA BORENSTEIN: ?] We [INAUDIBLE] and rather happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And very much in love.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you still? [? SHEILA BORENSTEIN: ?] Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got married in Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: They got married three times, once in Siberia when my father gave him [NON-ENGLISH], and then when he came to Israel. [? SHEILA BORENSTEIN: ?] Not Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [? SHEILA BORENSTEIN: ?] Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to-- we had to have proof of being married. And we didn't have it. So this way, we had to get married. And two children were the witnesses [INAUDIBLE].

words.text: this way, we had to get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the third time? [? SHEILA BORENSTEIN: ?] Yeah, it was very funny.

speaker: interviewee
text: The third time is a year ago in Delray Beach in Florida-- [? SHEILA BORENSTEIN: ?] Temple Emeth. --in Temple Emeth. 23 couples celebrated the 50th anniversary. It was a regular wedding with a chuppah with-- [? SHEILA BORENSTEIN: ?] Gold. Everything in gold. With breaking the glass and with everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the vows and [INAUDIBLE]. And we're happy to be as a family together. And-- [? SHEILA BORENSTEIN: ?] [INAUDIBLE]--

speaker: interviewee
text: Many, many more. [? SHEILA BORENSTEIN: ?] --for many more years.

speaker: interviewee
text: As you can see, we are very close family. We're us-- me and my brother and his wife, my wife, my sister, and my other brother-- we always try to be together.

speaker: interviewee
text: We even live in Florida together within about a mile of each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, in fact, I should've been here in America in 1946. And I came in 1956, because to be together with my family to Israel. [? SHEILA BORENSTEIN: ?] OK, that's enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is one of the earliest pictures of my family showing my parents, Sender Borenstein, my mother, Chava Borenstein, my oldest brother, George.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's on the right-hand side. And my sister, Shaindl, who I've never met, she was born and died long before I was born. And my brother, Sam, on the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is approximately in 1920 maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that behind them, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's just the scenery at the studio. OK. This is a picture-- family picture showing my mother on the right, Chava Borenstein, a cousin, Bayla Borenstein, who was lost in the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How is she related?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was my father's brother's daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's your father's brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- Israel, Israel. And on the right-hand side, the little girl is my sister, Faigele. And in the middle is my brother Sam. And next to him, my brother Jacob-- or Yakob, as we used to call him. And down at the bottom-- it's me when I was about, I guess, two or three years old. The picture was taken, figure, about 1928. And this picture survived having been buried in the ground and went through Siberia and all of our history. It's one of our dearest possessions.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken most likely in the suburb of Warsaw in Otwock, on in a summer place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: O-T-W-O-C-K. Otwock. OK, this is a picture of my mother's sister, Chaya Mindel.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's on--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which side?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left-hand side, looking at a portrait of my grandmother, or their mother, Esther Faigele. And my sister's named after her. And she died the same day my sister was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother. As a matter of fact, she said, wait before you name your daughter. That's what she said to my mother. Because she's going to be my name. And she died the same day that she was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture's most likely to taken in Israel where my aunt, Chaya Mindel, lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is she still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She's long gone. This is one of the early pictures, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say, oh, many years-- I don't know-- long, long before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: The '20s?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the '20s. Yeah, probably in the '20s. This is a picture of my parents, Sender and Chava Borenstein, taken before the war, probably in the '30s, early '30s. And it looks like either the park in Warsaw or during their summer vacation. I'm not quite sure. They used to go every summer to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] either a-- I don't know-- one of those rest places that they'd--

speaker: interviewer
text: A spa?

speaker: interviewee
text: The spas, right. This might be [NON-ENGLISH]. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, yeah. OK. This is a picture of my brother Jacob taken shortly before the war, I would say about 1938. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He's the one that saved the family by rescuing us and taking us out-- out of Warsaw into Russia. And he himself perished when he tried to rescue more people after he went-- tried to go back to Poland and bring back more people and was caught by the Russians and sent to Archangelsk where he perished in 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old?

speaker: interviewee
text: --December 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was 24 years old at the time-- at the time he died. OK. This is a picture of my parents, Sender and Chava Borenstein, in front of our cafe. This was taken-- taken in 1938, I'm almost sure. I think I remember being there when the picture was taken. The sign above the store, which by the way was made by my brother Sam, who was a sign painter. It says, [NON-ENGLISH]. That means, beer to be consumed in the place or to take out. And on the right-hand side above, it says, Lody, ice cream. We were very famous for ice cream. We made it ourselves. We had our own ice cream machine. And eventually, my brother, who was an electrician, designed an automated motorized ice cream machine. And we made them in different flavors-- vanilla, chocolate, and other fruit flavors. The signs on the side of the panels on the store says, [NON-ENGLISH]. This is a famous name of a beer company that was before the war and famous in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the address?

speaker: interviewee
text: The address is 116 Leszno Street-- L-E-S-Z-N-O Street, in Warsaw. Right across the-- from the store was the [NON-ENGLISH], one of the famous shopping areas in this part of Warsaw. It brings back fond memories. We all used to work in the place. Everybody had to partake in-- in-- in operating the business from the oldest to the youngest, including myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of myself and my wife, who was my girlfriend at the time. It was taken approximately in 1953, just before we got married, and more or less an engagement picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brooklyn, New York. And that's about it. OK. This is a picture of our middle daughter, Janet Renee, married to Roy Schmukler, right next to her, and the whole family at the time-- myself, next to Janet.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're in the middle in the top row?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm in the middle. And on my right is my oldest brother, George. And just below him, his wife, [? Fanny. ?] Next to her, my wife, Esther. And in the middle is my youngest daughter, Penny Ellen. And at the very bottom is Cheryl Renee-- Cheryl Ann, rather, our oldest daughter. And going to right, the extreme right on top, is my brother-in-law, Leon, and my sister, Faigele, right below him. And next to them is my brother Sam with his wife [? Sheila. ?] This was taken in 1976 when Janet was married, our middle daughter's wedding. OK. This is a picture of my daughter, Janet Renee, with her husband, Roy Schmukler, and our three grandchildren-- Jacob Alexander, on the-- below Roy. In the middle is the Zehava Shoshana. And on the right is Eliahu Baruch Peretz. These are the Orthodox children. And now, the Conservative family. That's my oldest daughter, Cheryl Ann, with her husband, Yuri Katz. And Yuri is holding the youngest son, Daniel Evan. And Cheryl's holding Michael Philip. This is our family. And our other daughter, Penny, is not in this picture. This is our youngest daughter-- OK, this is our youngest daughter, Penny-- Penny Ellen. She was born in 1960. This picture was taken a couple of years ago. It was a graduation picture when she graduated from medical school. Our recording today was primarily made at her prodding. She always wanted to-- to know my history, the background of family events and how we survived the Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And our message is also directed to her, the message that I left on the tape before. OK. This is a copy of a family tree from my mother's side. It's called the Minkendorf Descendants. It shows my grandparents, my five-- four aunts and one uncle. And it also shows the middle branch that was the Holocaust-- that perished in the Holocaust, the whole family of the Polish family and the other side, the Minkendorfs and the Dorfs and the family from Israel, the Chaya Mindel Horowitz. And unfortunately, the girls that perished-- I don't remember their names. And the rest of the family from my mother's side are on the-- listed on this tree.
